title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Fault system geometry is complex



Western North America has 100,000+ km of imperfectly (look where the faults stop) mapped fault system
Fractal dimensions range from 1.1 (Okubo and Aki, 1987) to 1.6 (Hirata, 1989)










USGS Quaternary fault and fold database

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Fault system kinematics are countable


\(>90\%\) of shortening is localized on 1-5 faults at active convergent mountain belts









Geology and geodesy seem to have identified the faults responsible for \(>90\%\) of kinematic motion in strike-slip systems 



Minster and Jordan, 1987; Lave and Avouac, 2001; Meade and Hager, 2005

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
section: Modern geodetic observations show astonishing localization
text:
Modern geodetic observations show astonishing localization







Wright et al., 2026

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
section: Steady linear mantle flow is not geodetically detectable
text:
Steady linear mantle flow is not geodetically detectable
Steady flow in a linear* viscous mantle produces a stress at the crust-mantle interface and the elastic layer responds with strain, not strain rate
\[\sigma_\mathrm{e} = \sigma_\mathrm{v}\;\;\; \rightarrow \;\;\;\mu \epsilon_\mathrm{e} = \eta \frac{d\epsilon_\mathrm{v}}{dt}\]

…geodetic observations of interseismic deformation are insensitive to the source of the driving forces in a linear system (Savage, 2000)


…motion in the elastic layer is decoupled from that deep within the viscous layer, and they may be very different from each other (Zatman, 2000)


We also demonstrate that the geodetic velocities are not related to the secular velocities at depth (Hetland and Hager, 2004)



*Non-linear rheologies make this history dependent and open up the door to connections

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
section: Some transient mantle flow is geodetically detectable
text:
Some transient mantle flow is geodetically detectable















1-10 year postseismic VE deformation is well documented
~100 year postseismic VE deformation is yet to documented
Maxwell VE rheology is incompatible with observations
Extended linear rheologies (e.g., Burgers and Andrade) linear and many nonlinear rheologies are consistent with observations




Hetland and Hager 2005, 2006, Meade et al., 2013

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Modern perspectives on friction (geodesy)
Afterslip in the “wrong” place




inconsistent with a simple model consisting of velocity-weakening patches obeying simple rate-state friction surrounded by a velocity strengthening interface (Johnson et al., 2012)

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Modern perspectives on friction (geodesy)
Little geodetically observed surface fault creep




Potential solution: frictional parameters are velocity/state/stress/history dependent rather than material properties

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Modern perspectives on friction (laboratory)





We therefore argue here that earthquake dynamics are best understood in terms of dynamic fracture mechanics and not governed by the frictional properties of faults…We finally note that any process that includes shear and normal stresses can be interpreted in terms of “friction” (Reches and Fineberg, 2023)

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
section: Slip displacement, stress, strain energy distance scaling
text:
Slip displacement, stress, strain energy distance scaling



\[
U_{ij} = \frac{-1}{8\pi(1-\nu)r^2} \left\{ \frac{\partial r}{\partial n} \left[ (1-2\nu)\delta_{ij} + \frac{3r_i r_j}{r^2} \right] - (1-2\nu)\left[ \frac{n_i r_j}{r} -  \frac{n_j r_i}{r} \right] \right\}
\]













Kinematics are long distance. Energy is localized.

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
section: Stress is non-trivial to calculate in fractured media
text:
Stress is non-trivial to calculate in fractured media



Hypersingular for Dirac delta function* point source. No known general integration method
Linear elastic fracture mechanics (LEFM) works with weaker singularities
Stress free formulations of continuum mechanics have been developed










*High order continuity (Mallick and Meade, 2024) and mollification (Eringen, 1977) can fix this

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
section: Stress and force as distributions or computational tools
text:
Stress and force as distributions or computational tools

Stress is very difficult to measure
Displacement and energy are clear are clearly interpretable








Wilcek, 2004

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
section: The crust may be jammed and/or mathematically stiff
text:
The crust may be jammed and/or mathematically stiff












Slow loading with itermittent jumps (Anderson, 1975)
Jamming mechanics (Liu and Nagel, 1998)
Crackling noise (Niccolini et al., 2009)
Contact dynamics (Moreau, 1999)




Sieh et al., 2008

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
A micro summary

1000s of geologic slip rate estimates and 1m+ geodetic observations suggest that in the modern most motion is localized on an effectively countable number of faults despite fractal network geometry





No geodetically detectable relationship between crustal motions to mantle motions (for linear rheologies, unclear for non-linear)





Friction may not be a primary control on failure styles





Stress and strain are powerful calculation tools but are often difficult to work with when there is faulting/fracture





If the crust is in a jammed state it is highly sensitive to small perturbations but geometrically constrained

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Paths forward: Kinematics + statistical mechanics



Rationale for thermodynamics

The principle of the production of [motion by heat]…must be considered independently of any mechanism or…particular agent. It is necessary to establish principles applicable not only to [steam-engines] but to all imaginable [heat engines] (Carnot, 1824)





Rationale for faulting

The principle of the production of [fault slip] must be considered independently of any mechanism or…particular agent. It is necessary to establish principles applicable not only to [elasticity, rate/state friction] but to all imaginable [rheologies]

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Paths forward: Kinematics + statistical mechanics
For earthquake and afterslip sequences:

Moment budgets: A kinematic balance concept
Empirical scaling laws: What we can measure
Maximum entropy: Everything else is maximally uncertain








Meade, in review

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Paths forward: Geometry dominated

Plate geometries have granular coordination numbers (Anderson, 2001)
Fault geometry changes have a crustal thickness length scale (Klinger, 2010)
Environmental soft matter is a statistical cube (Domokos et al., 2020)

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Paths forward: Non-linear viscous models

Many non-linear rheologies (e.g., power-law) create more informative systems
Pipe diffusion, \(\eta = f(\sigma, \epsilon, ...)\) (Breithaupt et al., 2022)

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Paths forward: Discrete element models

Inertial (Cundall and Strack, 1979) and non-inertial (Kishino, 1988)
Decide with the inertial number \(I = \dot{\epsilon}d \sqrt{\rho / P}\)

Inertial non-inertial threshold \(I \approx 10^{-2}\)
For the Earth-like tectonics \(I \approx 10^{-12}\)

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Paths forward: Contact dynamics

Moreau, 1987; Andrews et al., 2022
Used widely in animation and robotics
Non-inertial, polygonal discrete elements, with deformable bodies
Non-convex. Non-smooth. Geometry determines if a solution exists

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Paths forward: Neohookean elasticity

Large deformation fractures used in animation and surgery simulations
Emperical relationships to micromechanical properties








sofa-framework.org

title: Some aspects of understanding and modeling crustal motions
text:
Paths forward

Fracture/fault first models address primary crustal observables





The evolution of fault system geometry may be a combination of historical contingency, geometric constraints, and non-monotonic fracture evolution





Frontiers and needs:

Non-signular fracture mechanics and energy focused models
New thinking about “material properties”
Models that explain observations of jammed and stiff motion (little creep, neglible long-term transients)
Non-linear (constraints and/or geometry and/or rheology) and sampled models
